subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The chapter's organising purpose is discovering and describing rules in numbers: students identify supercell patterns (§3.2), digit-sum regularities (§3.4), palindrome formation and the reverse-and-add procedure (§3.5), convergence to the Kaprekar constant (§3.6), Collatz sequences (§3.10), number-line patterns (§3.3), and winning-number patterns in games (§3.12) — all requiring the student to find, articulate, and generalise a rule from a number-based structure.

Sections 3.3, 3.8, and 3.11 form a structurally independent strand: students place large multi-digit numbers accurately on number lines (§3.3), perform and reason about 5-digit additions and subtractions using mental strategies (§3.8), and estimate real-world magnitudes ranging from steps walked to continental distances (§3.11), directly exercising the ability to compare, estimate, and operate on large whole numbers.

adjunct_competencies.justification: The Kaprekar process (§3.6) and the Collatz procedure (§3.10) both require students to apply a precisely defined iterative algorithm — execute a step, check a condition, repeat — embodying the computational thinking capacities of iteration and algorithmic thinking explicitly named in C-8.1.

Section 3.1 asks students to justify whether particular height-neighbour sequences are achievable; §3.2 requires proving why the largest number must always be a supercell and why a table with no supercells is impossible; §3.12 requires identifying a game's winning strategy through conjectured patterns and logical argument — all exercises in constructing and evaluating mathematical proofs.

Sections 3.7, 3.9, and 3.12 explicitly invite students to create their own puzzle variations, design number-pattern arrangements summing to a chosen target, and discover winning strategies for self-invented game rules — sustained creative problem-making rather than solution of prescribed exercises.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to discover and describe rules governing number patterns by engaging with structured number games, digit-based phenomena, iterative algorithms, and estimation challenges that each demand the recognition and articulation of a generalisable principle.
